summary: Gruber responds to criticism of his earlier question about how Android supporters reconcile their 'winning' narrative with Sprint's need for the iPhone. He clarifies that by 'winning' he means the theory that Android will do to mobile what Windows did to PCs in the 1990s — effectively destroying all competition. He argues this isn't happening because the iPhone commands two-thirds of mobile industry profits, making it indispensable to carriers in a way the Mac never was to computer stores. His core thesis is that there is no Windows-like monopoly winner in mobile; instead, Android and iOS are succeeding in fundamentally different ways, playing different games entirely.
key_points: The 'Android is winning' narrative implies Android will replicate Windows' 1990s dominance, reducing iOS to niche irrelevance — Gruber argues this isn't happening
The iPhone accounts for two-thirds of mobile phone industry profits, making it essential to carriers in a way the Mac never was to PC retailers
Market share without profit share is a Pyrrhic victory — Windows dominated both metrics, while Android only leads in unit sales
Google and Apple are playing fundamentally different games with Android and iOS, making direct market share comparisons misleading
Google's purchase of Motorola suggests Google is more interested in playing Apple's game than vice versa
money_quotes: When I say Android isn't 'winning' I don't mean it isn't doing well or isn't growing, I mean that it isn't relegating iOS to a '90s Mac-sized slice of the market.
Mobile carriers need the iPhone today. Computer stores did not need the Mac.
Money is how you keep score, because it's the one thing whose value everyone agrees upon. That's what money is.
Market share without profit is a Pyrrhic victory.
I'm not arguing that iOS is the new Windows; I'm arguing that there is no Windows in mobile.
Both platforms are succeeding, albeit in very different ways.
With its purchase of Motorola, Google seems a lot more interested in playing Apple's game than the other way around.
key_insight: The mobile market has no Windows-like winner because Android and iOS are playing fundamentally different games — Android dominates unit sales while Apple captures the vast majority of profits, and profit share is the metric that actually matters.
